sentences.text: Well, after they killed my sister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my older sister, I didn't know where she was.
She was with the partisans.
I was all alone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got at some food and went back to the Warsaw ghetto and tried to get my little sister out.
[INAUDIBLE]
Leah.
My little sister, [INAUDIBLE], she was old at nine, but my little sister.
And I came to the ghetto like I always did, looked around.
And something was wrong.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Instead of having mostly Ukrainians and Polish soldiers, Polish police, now there were a lot of Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The street car didn't go through.
Something was wrong.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a few hours, walking around trying to find out what happened, I found out that the ghetto was closed, which means you can get in.
You can't get out, even if you are an official or whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided to give the food away.
No use carrying it get back to the village.
I went back to the village.
Now, I know what it was.
At that time, I didn't know.
This was the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising.
What did that mean?
At that time, I didn't know.
It didn't mean nothing to me.
No, everybody was in the ghetto.
Nothing disappeared.
There were people in the ghetto, but even the officials could not get in or out.
Even the Germans were not allowed to get in and out.
That's how the ghetto was closed.
So to me, it meant something was wrong.
But what?
I don't know.
Now, I know.
Now, I also know the day.
I did know then.
I mean, they-- they didn't mean anything.
The only date I knew is that it's light, and I got to eat something.
Then it's dark, and you go to sleep.
It didn't mean anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So now, I know it was the uprising.
It was Passover night, Pesach night, 1943.
I remember now.
It was April 15.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the reason I remember it now is because I was told.
At that time, you didn't even think about Pesach.
You didn't think about anything.
The only thing you thought of is a German is on this side, you-- you were away on the other side.
I went back to the village where I was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was taken by-- taken in by a priest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to more communions, I made more Hail Marys, then most Catholics do in a lifetime.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to.
The priest said, do you want to be saved?
Do you want to be a Catholic?
You gotta do it.
And I went to communion.
I even went to confession.
Now, I know.
I didn't know what confessional was.
What do I know about communion?
For me to say, at that time, Hail Mary, full of grace, what do I know what it means?
But I said it in order to live.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how long I was there.
What happened to Leah and your mother?
I don't know.
Never saw them again.
They were left.
When I went to get her from the ghetto, they were left.
And I couldn't get in.
I couldn't get them out.
Never got in.
Luckily, I'm alive.
If I would've got in, I wouldn't have been alive.
That was the day when the ghetto was doomed.
The people in the ghetto was doomed.
And until today, I don't know.
Nobody knows.
It's like they didn't exist-- funny, you know, like they didn't exist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just went back to Poland, because the United States government needed a birth certificate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I wrote to the United States Embassy in Warsaw telling them where I lived, where I was born.
And they said, go ahead and send $18, and we'll get you birth certificate.
I said, beautiful, fine.
Because I would never go back to Poland.
They don't deserve me, to have me over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I got the letter back about three months later from the embassy, saying, we are sorry to inform you-- I still have the letter.
We are sorry to inform you that according to the Polish government, we have no papers.
And you-- you don't exist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, that got me mad.
Because I do exist.
And I am alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So you said, you know, where are they?
Where do they exist?
I don't know.
So I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a little revenge when I went back to them in 1993, and she told me, you don't exist.
I said to her in Polish, touch me.
And she's says, why?
I said, so you know that I exist.
I'm here.
I lived two blocks from here.
You know where it is.
She says, I'm sorry.
We don't have any papers on you.
I said, you don't have to.
I exist.
It's fine.
That's a little revenge, of saying, I exist.
But it doesn't take care of much.
So when I was at this priest's house, I worked.
It wasn't bad.
And then--
Must have been.
It was '43, middle of '43, end of '43.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess he ran out of vodka.
I guess so.
He turned me in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was working for him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, the farmers over there, they have, like, 15, 20 farms, all the way around, you know?
And the middle is a church, you know, which is fine.
He was like the head of the village and the priest and everything.
But I guess he ran out of vodka.
So one day, the Germans showed up.
And he says, there he is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there I was.
And they took me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Why he turned me in, I don't know.
But he did.
I remember being taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by cattle car.
And right now, in '43, '44, the memory gets a little better, not too much.
I was taken to a place called Breslau, which is just on the German border.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I was taken to a place called Leipzig, which is not too far away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I was taken to a place called Halle, which is not too far away also.
It's on the same road.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot to tell you that when I was taken in, they asked me what my name is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And even though my name is Brzezowski, which is Polish, sounds Polish, spelled Polish, I told them it was Valdimar Kozlowski.
Why Valdimar Kozlowski?
Because I told them I was a volksdeutsche.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I speak German.
I spoke German then also.
I was a volksdeutsche.
And by a volksdeutsche, it means that you can get by with a lot of things.
You are one of them, almost.
So there I was now, Valdimar Kozlowski, taken to Breslau, Leipzig, Halle.
Halle was one of the worst.
Halle was a prison.
It was not a arbeitslager.
It was not a camp.
You didn't go to work there.
You were just going to prison, period.
No, prisons.
But you-- but you had to go to work from the prison.
They would take you out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would take you out to clean the streets, to do all kinds of things in the city.
Because they didn't have any people there, any manpower.
So they would have one old man with a rifle that was bigger than he was.
And he didn't look like almighty God, himself.
But he still had a rifle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we worked.
We cleaned, and we did all kinds of things.
Halle was the worst.
Because Halle, they were coming in more often.
And they would line us up in the morning, every morning, for roll call.
Like, how many people were still there?
And they would say, line up, and call out, one, two, one, two, one, two, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the bad thing was that you didn't know what number to stand under.
Because one day they would take number two-- take number two away.
He says, all number two, go over there.
The next day, all number one.
And you never saw them again.
So you don't know where they did, where they went.
Did they die?
Were they killed?
You didn't know.
So the worst thing was in the morning when you got up, which one to be-- number one, or number two.
That was the worst.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how long it lasted.
It must have been an eternity.
From there-- Were you with any friends?
No.
Totally alone.
And to be honest with you, I don't even know if the people were Jewish or not Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
I don't remember.
We talked Polish.
I wouldn't dare speak a Jewish word.
And if they were Jewish, they didn't either.
So I don't know.
But from there, we were taken to a place called Buchenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just found out where it is.
It's in Weimar.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So Buchenwald, at that time-- I guess all the time, I don't know-- was like a way station for people to go to work.
If you were able to go to work, they sent you places to go to work.
If you were not able, it's too bad.
You were dead, period.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I was in barrack eight.
How was it when you arrived?
Do you remember?
It was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means, fast, fast, fast.
The older people right, younger people left.
I was to the left.
I was on a train that were mostly men, not women.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were taken then into a place where we had to change clothes, put- put the black-and-white garbs on, shaven.
Their heads were shaven.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, they tell me where it was.
Now, it's-- now, it is a museum.
But they tell me it was a laundry mat, a laundry place.
I don't remember there was a laundry place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe there was, but I don't remember.
I remember where I was.
I remember all the barracks.
I remember where they were.
The barracks.
I remember where the Germans were.
I remember where their barracks were.
I remember where we were killed, where the crematorium was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the stench.
[CRYING] Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how long I was there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But it must have been a long time, a lifetime.
It took me almost 50 years to open my mouth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If my daughter didn't insist, on the-- in April of 1993, going to see where I come from, I would've never opened my mouth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would have died with it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] never talked to my kids, never talked to my wife, never talked to my friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Right now, the last two years, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I almost devoted my life.
That one child is going to know what I went through, one person.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I speak a few times a year at Temple Isaiah, which I belong to, to teenagers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They write letters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I speak sometimes to a college, to older people.
I think it's time that they know, that everybody knows-- not what went on, but what I went through.
And maybe, maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Maybe it'll help.
It took me 50 years.
And the only reason I went is because my daughter paid for it.
I would have never gone.
To Europe, to Poland and Germany.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's not there anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
We would-- it was--
Oh, there were a lot of people there.
No.
And if you did talk to somebody, you didn't want to get friendly.
Because you could have been killed the next day.
And then where you are?
And then you say, gee, thank God it was him.
It wasn't me.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You see, now you know when you see an army movie, and the soldiers start seeing it where his friend died right next to him, they say, gee, by God.
For the grace of God, there go I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know how they feel.
[CRYING] It's not just by the grace of God, I guess.
I don't know.
I don't know.
But you didn't want to have anything to do with anybody.
Because if you had something to do, and if you picked up a peel of a potato someplace, you had to share it.
And you were so selfish at that time.
You didn't want to share it.
You wanted to eat it, because you wanted to survive.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Sometimes I feel guilty.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was God there to help me survive?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In one way, I'll tell you.
If there was a God, why did he let all that happen?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The same bet, I can tell you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe he was there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe.
Maybe he saved me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I got a dilemma.
And I don't know how to solve it, but I'm alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sometimes I feel it's him.
Sometimes when I think of all the other people that went before me, I don't think so.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I guess that's me.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
We-- and one day I was taken out of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Buchenwald, taken to a arbeitslager, a place called Rothenburg on the Tauber.
That I remember.
You asked me if I got together with anybody.
I got together with one guy who taught me Italian.
He was the Italian prisoner of war.
And we-- we came in into this camp, just a working camp, no concentration camp.
By that, I mean no gas chamber or something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were building a bunker in a huge hill to put a factory in there to build airplanes or parts, I guess.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rothenburg is up on a hill.
The Tauber, which is a big river, is on the bottom.
And what we had to do is fill up pushcarts of stones from the-- from the hole and push it down to the river, to the Tauber, fill it up, pull it downstream a few kilometers, unload it, come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every time you went down the hill, you'd go up the hill.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our rations was-- I mean, when I came out of camp, I was 90 pounds.
I'll tell you what it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, it's a beautiful town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody knows nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was there I don't know how long.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that wasn't bad.
You just got beat up if you didn't work hard.
But it wasn't-- you-- you expected to live so long as you pushed that push cart and you dug those stones out.
But you were not killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there I was taken to-- one day they came in with a-- loaded all of us up and put us into train.
And it must have been 10 years, I guess, we were in that train.
I don't know how long it was, going back and forth and back and forth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we each had a spoon that you got your soup with to eat.
So when we left, we took the tools with us.
So some of us knocked on the spoon at the edge with a stone until it became sharp.
And every day or so, they would stop and open the trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a bucket over there to go to the bathroom.
Same bucket was used for water.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So a few of us decided to cut it out, to escape.
How do you escape?
You don't know where you are.
You don't know what you're doing.
But we figured it can't be worse than it is now.
It's got to be the end, so might as well do it.
And we started to cut the boards from that train with that spoon.
The spoon became sharp.
It was like a knife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we cut out a board.
And one night, we started to jump out.
All of us in the-- in the train, in the cattle car, a lot of us.
I didn't know by name who they were.
But there were lots of us.
It was packed.
And on each train, there was a German soldier with a machine gun.
We didn't know that.
And as the train passed by, and they saw people jumping off, they would just shoot at random.
A lot of people died.
I was shot-- shot in my leg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Luckily, I don't know why, but the bullet didn't go all the way in.
I was able to take it out with my hands.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My foot became gangrene later on.
But when we jumped off the train, we found out where we were.
We were in-- on the Czechoslovakian border called Teplice-Sanov.
That I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there, there were a lot of us, different stories to tell to the farmers.
We wanted work, just to get out of the way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember lying for two days outside this village.
There was, like, a brook of water lying right there.
It was fresh water.
I put the water on my leg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you had to do away with some of the clothing that you had.
Like, we all had to have the-- the black and white cap.
We did not have-- we did not have black and white clothes.
But we had the black and white cap.
We had no shoes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember being taken in by the burgomeister.
